coredata.dc:title: A high-sensitive ratiometric luminescent thermometer based on dual-emission of carbon dots/Rhodamine B nanocomposite
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Zhenpeng
Sun, Zishan
Guo, Ziying
Zhang, Xinguo
Wu, Zhan-chao
coredata.dc:description: A core-shell nanocomposite based on carbon dots (CD)/Rhodamine B (RhB) is realized by a facile method. The composite particles show spherical shape with narrow size distribution, non-agglomeration and smooth surface. The as-obtained nanocomposite shows the characteristic dual-emission of thermal-stable CD blue emission and thermal-sensitive RhB red emission at a single excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) is found to have a good linear relationship (R 2 > 0.993) with temperature, while the corresponding maximum absolute and relative sensitivity is 2.01% K−1 and 1.39% K−1 in range of 283–373 K. The research provides a simple approach to create novel dual-emitting nanocomposites for temperature sensing application in micron-scale and biological field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rhodamine
Nanocomposite
Luminescence
Thermometer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB (1) Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD (2) Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2 (3) Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB.
(a) UV–Vision spectra and (b) FT-IR spectra of different samples; (c) TGA of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB.
XPS spectra of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD (a) Full XPS, (b) Gd3d, (c) O1s, (d) N1s, (e) C1s, (f) Gd4d.

(a), (b) and (c) are the SEM, enlarged SEM and TEM images of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD; (d), (e) and (f) are the SEM, enlarged SEM and TEM images of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2; (g), (h) and (i) are the SEM, enlarged SEM and TEM images of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB.
(a) the SEM image of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD; (b) the size distribution of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD; (c) and (d) the TEM images of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD.
(a) the TEM image of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2; (b) the size distribution of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2; (c) and (d) the enlarged TEM images of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2.
(a) the TEM image of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB; (b) the size distribution of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB; (c) and (d) the enlarged TEM images of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB.
Photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of (a) Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD and Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2; (b) Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB.

(a) the PL spectra of the Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB and attenuation map of fluorescence intensity in the temperature range of 283–373 K. (b) Linear relationship between the ratios of the integral area of fluorescence intensity(FIR = I 595 nm/I 450 nm). (c) Ratio(I 595 nm/I 450 nm) of sample fluorescence intensity in temperature cycling test.
CIE coordinates of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB at the temperature range of 293–373 K.
MTT results of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB at different concentrations.
Fitting parameters of the decay lifetime in Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB system.

row.entry: Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD (λ ex = 320 nm λ em = 450 nm)
Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2 (λ ex = 320 nm λ em = 450 nm)
Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB (λ ex = 320 nm λ em = 450 nm)
Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB (λ ex = 320 nm λ em = 590 nm)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of temperature sensing range, absolute and relative sensitivity of the representative luminescent ratiometric thermometers.

head.ce:title: A high-sensitive ratiometric luminescent thermometer based on dual-emission of carbon dots/Rhodamine B nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-Chemical Engineering
Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Applied Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhenpeng Zhu and Zishan Sun contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-sensitivity and colormetric temperature sensing is realized by nanocomposite with carbon dots and Rhodamine B.

A core-shell nanocomposite based on carbon dots (CD)/Rhodamine B (RhB) is realized by a facile method. The composite particles show spherical shape with narrow size distribution, non-agglomeration and smooth surface. The as-obtained nanocomposite shows the characteristic dual-emission of thermal-stable CD blue emission and thermal-sensitive RhB red emission at a single excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) is found to have a good linear relationship (R 2 > 0.993) with temperature, while the corresponding maximum absolute and relative sensitivity is 2.01% K−1 and 1.39% K−1 in range of 283–373 K. The research provides a simple approach to create novel dual-emitting nanocomposites for temperature sensing application in micron-scale and biological field.

ce:section.ce:para: High-sensitive and accurate temperature measurement is of great importance in everyday life and multiple industrial fields. Conventional thermometers such as expansion thermometer, thermocouple thermometer and infrared thermometer could not satisfy the future measurement requirements of non-contact, high resolution and fast response. Thus, developing the non-contact temperature sensors with submicron scale, high sensitivity and temperature resolution and quick response has garnered a lot of attention while remains a great challenge for researchers [1,2].

Using temperature-dependent optical properties of luminescent materials, optical thermometer represents a potentially useful tool in micro electronic devices, biology, and medical diagnostics [3–6]. Recently, many optical thermometers have been developed, such as fluorescent dyes, quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), phosphors, metal nanoclusters (NCs) [7,8], graphene [9,10] and graphdiyne [11,12]. Among them, CDs have attracted increasing attention and have been widely used as probes in chemical detection [13] and stimuli responsive system [14], because of the merits such as low toxicity, facile preparation, no photo-bleaching, good biocompatibility and high quantum yield [15]. However, most carbon-dot-based temperature sensors rely on fluorescence quenching processes, which could be easily influenced by many factors such as fluorescence efficiency, excitation intensity, and probe concentration [16–18]. As is well-known, ratiometric fluorescence thermometer, which is based on the temperature-dependent florescence intensity ratio (FIR) of two distinct emission peaks, has a unique advantage of being independent of above-mentioned factors, while owns high resolution and small measurement error [19–25].Therefore, it is highly desirable for designing novel CDs-based ratiometric temperature sensors that possess both strong emission and submicron scale.

In this work, a dual-emitting sphere-like nanocomposite based on carbon dots/Rhodamine B combination (Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB) is designed and constructed by a green and facile reaction. A molecular level distribution of blue-emitting cys-CD is achieved by incorporated cys-CD into Gd(OH)CO3 nano-sphere, which could inhibit aggregation-induced quenching of CDs and result in a thermal-stable blue emission as an internal. Meanwhile, RhB dye, whose red emission exhibits regular quenching with evaluating temperature, is absorbed by mesopores silica layer and act as a probe [26]. The as-prepared composite can be utilized for ratiometric fluorescent probe using variation of blue and red emission intensity ratio, which shows a linear relationship with temperature and result in a high-sensitive temperature measurement in wide range of 283–373 K.

l-cystine (cys), sodium citrate, urea, Gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate (CAS: 19598–90-4 Gd(NO3)3·6H2O), TEOS (tetraethoxysilane), CTAB (Cetyltrimethyl Ammonium Bromide), acetone, ethanol, ethanediamine, rhodamine B were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai) and used as received. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

The synthesis of CD is a modification of preparation process in Ref. [27]. 0.2 g sodium citrate and 0.2 g l-cysteine were put in a clean beaker, where 10 mL ultrapure water was added for dissolving by 5 min ultrasonic treatment, then 500 μl ethylenediamine was dropped into the above solution before it transferred to a teflon reactor. The reactor was heated at 200 °C in a drying oven for 4 h. The reacted solution was put in a dialysis bag (Mw = 1000) when it reached room temperature, and after 48 h’ dialysis, the purified cys-CD solution was obtained.

In a typical method of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD synthesis [28], 5 mL Gd(NO3)3·6H2O(1 mol/L), 7.5 g urea and 75 mL cys-CDs solution were dispersed in 175 mL deionized water, and the mixture was stirred for 2 h at 25 °C and then heated in a 90 °C water bath for 2.5 h. Centrifugation (8000 rpm, 5 min) was used to collected the product and it was dried at 80 °C.

In the course of the experiment, the coating of the mesoporous silica (mSiO2) directly on the Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD is not successful after many trials. Therefore, the method of wrapping nonporous silica (nSiO2) and followed with mSiO2 is adopted by referring Zhao’s work [29]. 0.1 g dried Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD was dispersed in 80 mL ethanol by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. 20 mL deionized water, 1 mL 28 %wt NH3·H2O and 0.02 g TEOS was added into the suspension. Then the suspension was stirred for 6 h at room temperature and the obtained Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@nSiO2 was gathered by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 5 min) and washed by ethanol and water for several times.

The washed Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@nSiO2 was re-dispersed and stirred for 0.5 h in the solution composed of 80 mL water, 60 mL ethanol, 1 mL 28 %wt NH3·H2O and 0.3 g CTAB. 0.4 g TEOS was added subsequently before a 6 h stir at room temperature and Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@nSiO2 was coated by another silicon dioxide layer. The product was collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 5 min) and washed by ethanol and deionized water for 3 times. Dried Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@nSiO2@mSiO2 was dispersed in acetone and refluxed at 70 °C water bath for 24 h to generate mesopores silica layer by removing the template CTAB. The obtained sample was named as Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2.

600 mg dried Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2 was dispersed into 0.4 mg/mL RhB ethanol solution and stirred for 3 h. After washing by ethanol for 2 times and drying in the temperature, the dual-emitting luminescent nanoparticles Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB were obtained. The color of the final sample was changed from white to red under daylight, which indicates the successful absorption of RhB into mesoporous silica shell.

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of all samples were determined by a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. A small amount of the sample was dispersed in potassium bromide and sufficiently ground to form a uniform slice for the FT-IR measurement. The morphology and size of samples were observed by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7610F) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). For the TEM sample preparation, ∼0.05 g sample was dispersed in ethanol, after 10 min of sonication, 20 μl of the suspended droplets were dropped on a copper grid and dried naturally. The UV–vis spectra were performed by a Shimadzu UV-3600 ultraviolet spectrometer. Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) was recorded by a Thermal gravimetric analyzer (Netzsch TG209 F3). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD was explored by a X-ray Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (Kratos, Axis Ultra Dld). The photoluminescence/photoluminescence excitation (PL/PLE) spectra and fluorescence lifetime spectra were investigated by FLS-980 transient-steady-state fluorescence spectrometer using 150 W xenon lamp and 320 nm laser as excitation source, respectively. To figure out the temperature-dependent PL property, emission spectra in the range of 293–373 K with internal of 10 K were detected by using FLS-980 spectrometer with a liquid nitrogen cryostat (OptistatDN). Cytotoxicity test was detected by MTT assay with ELISPOT analyzer (CTL S6 Versa), to be specified, 100 μl of the cell suspension was prepared in a 96-well plate, and then the plate was pre-incubated in an incubator at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 24 h. Subsequently, 10 μl of different concentrations of final composite (12.5 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 200 μg/mL) of the test substance were added to the plate and the plate was incubated in the incubator for 24 h. 10 μl MTT solution was added to each well, and the plate was incubated in an incubator for 1–4 h, and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured with a microplate reader.

Excited by 320 nm laser, it is found that the Rhodamine B and cys-CDs decay lifetime in the nanocomposites was fitted by the following bi-exponential formula (1) [30]:
(1)
I
( t )
=
I 0
+
A 1
e x p
( -
t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
( -
t
τ 2
)
where I(t) represents emission intensity at 590 nm or 450 nm at time (t) while the initial intensity (t = 0) is I 0, A stands for a constant, and τ is the decay time for the exponential component. All fitting parameters are listed in Table 1 , and the average decay lifetime (τ* ) could be obtained by [30]:
(2)
τ
∗
=
∫
0
∞
t I
( t )
d t
∫
0
∞
t I
( t )
d t

Fig. 1 shows the synthesis scheme of the dual-emitting nanocomposite Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB. Typically, the synthetic route can be divided into three steps. Firstly, the cys-CD doping Gd(OH)CO3 nanospheres are synthesized as the mentioned hydrothermal method. Hereafter, the nanospheres are covered by nonporous and mesoporous silica layer. Finally, RhB dye is absorbed into the mesoporous layer and the Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB nanocomposite is obtained. The nanocomposite shows dual-emission and the intensity ratio of two emission peaks is linearly related to the changing temperature.

The UV–vis spectra of products in different steps are shown in Fig. 2 a. A sharp peak of 210 nm can be observed in the spectra of all the three samples, which is assigned to be the Gd3+–O2− charge transfer for Gd(OH)CO3 core. The peak appear at about 275 nm is assigning the 8 S 7/2-6 IJ transition of Gd3+. The absorption band at 290 and 370 nm originated from π → π* transitions of sp 2-conjugation in the CD core, and n → π* transition of N, S-doping at the CD surface, respectively [31]. The wide band at 555 of the characteristic absorption of RhB is only found in absorption spectrum of final sample Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB, revealing that the RhB had been encased in the mesoporous silica.

The FTIR spectra were measured to identify the composition and surface states of the as-prepared samples (Fig. 2b). The stretching vibrations of OH at 3410 cm−1, NH at 3200 cm−1 and CC at 1641 cm−1 are observed in the three as-prepared samples. The peak at 1695 cm−1 caused by CO of the carboxyl group of RhB is found in the spectrum of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB. Besides, the sharp peaks at about 1500 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to the v3 mode of carbonate while the three peaks around 1080 cm−1, 840 cm−1 and 750 cm−1 can be assigned to v1 , v2 and v4 modes of carbonate, respectively. In addition, the strong peak at about 1080 cm−1 and peaks around 760 cm−1 and 450 cm−1 are on account of the SiOSi anti-scaling vibration and SiO bond symmetrical stretching vibration, respectively. In general, the FT-IR analysis demonstrates that the particles are constituted by Gd, COOH, OH, SiO2, CO3 2− and bound H2O.

The elemental composition and its chemical states of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD were provided by XPS analysis. Total XPS spectrum in Fig. 3 a. reveals the existence of gadolinium (∼1185 eV), carbon (∼283 eV), nitrogen (∼398.5 eV), and oxygen (∼531 eV) atoms.[32] The high-resolution C1s, O1s, N1s, Gd3d and Gd4d of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD are also demonstrated. In Fig. 3c, peaks at 530.2 eV and 531.2 eV of O1s could be assigned to hydroxyl oxygen and carbonate oxygen, separately. As shown in Fig. 3d, the N1s spectrum have one peak at 398.9 eV, which could be attributed to Amino N. The C1s spectrum can be resolved as follows: 283.5 eV and 288.5 eV and 291.9 eV, demonstrating the exist of CC and COH and COOH (Fig. 3e) [33] The Gd3d spectrum in Fig. 3b shows an asymmetric peak at 1185.1 eV, the Gd4d spectrum (Fig. 3f) shows peaks at 142.1 eV and 151.0 eV, arising from a the state of Gd remains +3 in the Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD [34]. The N and C contained in the cys-CD come from the precursor ethylenediamine and sodium citrate, respectively. Both the spectrum of FT-IR and XPS indicates that the as-prepared Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD was consisted of multiple functional groups with oxygen, nitrogen and gadolinium elements, which illustrates that the cys-CD was successfully integrated in Gd(OH)CO3, making the Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD composite have the promising optical properties.

The TGA of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB composite was carried out to test its thermal stability and shown in Fig. 2c. The 7.53% of weight lose is observed at the beginning heating range from 30 °C to 150 °C revealing that the moisture of the sample was evaporated. Subsequently, the organic carbon in the CDs and RhB gradually decompose as the temperature reaches to 500 °C with 12.9% reduction of weight [35]. At last, Gd(OH)CO3 is transferred to Gd2O3 after the temperature achieved 600 °C and another 9.4% of weight loss is found at 800 °C [36]. The TGA result shows that Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB composite remains thermally stable at ∼500 °C.

The morphologies and structure of the as-prepared samples were identified by the SEM and TEM characterizations. Fig. 4 a and b present the SEM images of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD nanospheres prepared by hydrothermal method. It can be observed that the Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD sphere monodispersed with similar sizes of about 440 nm and smooth surface. These non-aggregated particles have a narrow size-distribution. The TEM image in Fig. 4c further confirms that Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD particle has a regular and exquisite spherical shape with smooth surface and size about 440 nm. The results indicate that the integration of cys-CDs hardly influences on the morphology and size distribution of Gd(OH)CO3 sphere because of the fairly small size of CDs.

It has been verified that mesoporous structure provides numerous advantages for multifunctional composite materials [37–39]. Therefore, a layer of silica was coated on the synthesized core Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD and formed mesoporous by refluxing with acetone. As the SEM images shown in Fig. 4d and e, the Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2 tightly next to each other are uniform both in shape and size. Due to the coated mesoporous silica, the spheres form comparatively rough surface and a core-shell structure is formed with diameter of ∼670 nm. Fig. 4f exhibits the further examination of the core-shell structure and morphology features. The core-shell structure consisting of a black Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD core with a size about 440 nm and a gray mesoporous silica layer wrapped around with a thickness about 115 nm is clearly seen from the TEM images. Mesoporous channels perpendicular to the surface of the spheres can be clearly found on the surface of silica shell, revealing that template CTAB was removed by refluxing with acetone so that the mesoporous silica shell was obtained.

Fig. 4g and h exhibit the SEM images of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB. After loading the RhB into the mesoporous silica layer, the morphology of the Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB still represents a uniform double-layer spherical morphology with a ∼670 nm diameter. According to the Fig. 4i, the core-shell structure of particles is still presented clearly and completely and both the size and thickness of the obtained composite are almost unchanged, demonstrating that the loaded RhB in the silica layer has no influence to the morphology. The particle size distribution maps and TEM images of samples are shown in Figs. 5–7
.

Fig. 8 a displays the PL and PLE spectra of both Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD and Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2. When monitoring at 450 nm emission peak, the PLE spectrum exhibits a broad absorption band at 370 nm, which can be assigned to the n → π* transition from CONH bond of cys-CDs. Meanwhile, the blue broadband emission at 450 nm is due to recombination of electron-hole pairs in the strongly localized π and π* electronic levels in graphitic structure of the carbon dots [20]. Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2 shows weaker intensity peaks at either excitation or emission because of the wrapped silica layer. Monitored by 450 nm emission, the PLE spectrum of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB shows a similar shape with Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD. Under single 370 nm excitation, both 450 nm blue emission from cys-CD and 590 nm red emission from RhB are simultaneous observed (Fig. 8b). Compared with the aqueous solution of RhB [40], the emission of solid-state RhB in the particles has a red-shift of about 40 nm, which might be attributed to its luminescence variation in the solid aggregate state.

The decay lifetimes of cys-CDs and RhB component monitored at each emission peak with UV laser excitation are shown in Fig. 9 . According to the formulas cited at Section 2.5, the τ * value of CD blue emission for Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD, Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2 and Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB was 5.68 ns, 6.12 ns, 5.70 ns, respectively. It can be seen that the lifetime of cys-CDs in presence and absence of RhB remained unaltered, indicating that the dye did not perturb the excited state of cys-CD. The lifetime of RhB was also measured to assess the excited state of the dye in composite. It is reported that the decay of RhB in aqueous solution was single exponential with average lifetime of 1.14 ns [41]. In present case, after RhB is incorporated into mesoporous silica of composite, the corresponding decay became bi-exponential consisting of a short and a long component with τ * of 1.69 ns. The shorter component of 1.10 ns might have originated from free RhB molecules on the composite surface, while the long component of 2.66 ns is probably due to the RhB molecules absorbed in mSiO2 layer.

Both temperature-dependent spectral shape and intensity have significant influence on the performance of luminescent thermometer. Different temperature response of dual emission is essential to realize a ratiometric thermometer. The temperature-dependent PL spectra of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB composite is measured and shown in Fig. 10 a. The nanocomposite exhibits a distinct dual-emission upon the excitation of 370 nm. Two peaks at 450 nm and 595 nm is corresponded to the emission of cysteine carbon dots and RhB respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 10a inset, the emission intensity of the cys-CDs at 450 nm is varied slightly with rising temperature from 283 to 373 K. Meanwhile, the corresponding intensity of RhB at 595 nm decreases gradually with rising temperature and drops to 40% of initial intensity at 373 K. The dropping emission intensity of RhB is attributed to the thermal-enhanced torsional movement of the terminal amino group in RhB structure, which in turn increases the internal transformation of S 1-S 0, and the rigid plane of the oxanthene parent ring is destroyed, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence quantum yield.

As an important parameter for exploring the new temperature sensors, the fluorescence intensity ratio FIR (I 595 nm/I 450 nm) is shown in Fig. 10b. The FIR gradually decreased with the increasing temperature, testifying the excellent linearity displayed in equation (3):
(3)
T = 440.6 - 48.68 ∗
I
590 n m
I
450 n m
,
R 2
= 0.9937

Stable temperature sensing is also an important part of the sensing properties, as a result, several temperature cycles measurement upon heating/cooling at 283 K and 373 K had been carried out and the result is listed in Fig. 10c. There is no obvious variation in temperature-dependent FIR value in more than three consecutive cycles, demonstrating its repeatability as optical thermometer. CIE coordinates of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB at the temperature range of 293–373 K are shown in the Fig. 11 . The emitting colors of the composite can be systematically varied in its temperature sensing ranges, which could be directly observed by the naked eye or the digital camera. Thus, it could act as sensitive colorimetric thermometer for real-time visualization.

In order to accurately evaluate the performance of the temperature sensor in quantitative terms, the absolute sensitivity (Sa ) and relative sensitivity (Sr ) had been calculated in accordance with the following equations [42]:
(4)
S a
=
∂ F I R
∂ T
(5)
S r
=
∂ F I R / ∂ T
FIR

Among the equations (4) and (5), represents the FIR (I595 nm /I450 nm ) while T is the temperature. According to the formula, our absolute sensitivity Sa is a constant with a value of 2.01% K−1 and the maximum relative sensitivity Sr with the value of 1.39% K−1 is found at 373 K. Simultaneously, it can be easily founded that the relative sensitivity Sr range is between 0.63% K−1 and 1.39% K−1 within the temperature of 283–373 K, which is more accurate than the previously reported nanoscale dual-emission thermometer (Fig. 12 a). As a comparison, Sa and Sr value of a few reported ratiometric fluorescent thermometers are displayed in Table 2 . Moreover, resolution, as a decisive parameter in the performance evaluation of a thermometer, can be estimated by using the equation below [43]:
(6)
δ T =
1
S r
δ Δ
Δ
where
δ Δ / Δ
stands for the experimental relative error which is determined by measurement device. In present case, a signal to noise ratio of 20,000: 1 is applied for FLS-980 spectrometer, which results in
δ Δ / Δ
of 0.01%. As seen in Fig. 12b, δT is always better than 0.1 K, illustrating this novel dual-emitting composite can be used as a potential luminescent thermometer.

In order to test whether the products is suitable for biological and medical research, a cytotoxicity test is applied by the MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 13 , when the concentration of Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB reaches at 12.5 μg/mL, the cells maintains a survival rate of 91.6%. And 53.1% of the cells remained active when the concentration is further increased to 200 μg/mL. Thus, this nanocomposite has a low cytotoxicity and could be applied in ratiometric optical thermometry in living cells.

In summary, an efficient dual-emitting nano-composite based on carbon dots/RhB is developed for ratiometric temperature sensing. The Gd(OH)CO3-cys-CD@mSiO2-RhB composite owns sphere morphology with core-shell structure, and displays single-excitation, dual-emission with two well-resolved, intensity-comparable fluorescence peaks. The temperature dependent PL emission spectra of the composite revealed that the cys-CD blue emission and RhB red emission has distinct temperature response, i.e. the former is thermal-stable and the latter exhibits quenching tendency, respectively. This results in highly temperature-dependent luminescence intensity ratio. With high sensitivity (S a = 2.01% K−1 and S r = 1.39% K−1), good index relationship between FIR and temperature (R 2 > 0.993), wide sensing range (283–373 K) with high resolution (>0.1 K), our developed composite exhibits higher accuracy than the previously reported nanoscale dual-emission thermometer [44–50], which could be a promising candidate for non-contact optical thermometry using a dual-color scheme. In subsequent studies, the size of the composite would be reduced by optimizing the synthesis conditions, and then the surface could be subjected to functional group modification or liposome encapsulation to make it target to the organelle. With a suitable particle size distribution for endocytosis, the organelle-targeting composite would have a better temperature sensing application in micron-scale and biological field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081). The authors thank Dr. Anxiang Guan (Laborotary of Advanced Materials, Department of Chemistry, Fudan University) and Dr. Fuwang Mo (College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hezhou University) for help in SEM and TEM measurement.
